markets.name: United States
markets.notes: The Datsun 620 was sold in the US from 1972-1979, marketed as the 'Li'l Hustler', arriving just before the 1973 oil crisis and becoming a key player in the compact pickup boom. Engine progression for the US market was L16 1.6L (96 hp, 1972-73), L18 1.8L (100 hp, 1974), then L20B 2.0L (110 hp gross, ~97 hp net in later years) from 1975-79. A 7-foot long bed on the 2785 mm wheelbase arrived in 1975, and the industry-first extended King Cab (cab stretched ~10 in / 24 cm, on the long chassis with the standard 6-ft bed) arrived for 1977. Swatch hex values are approximations from the colour names and period photos, not measured paint values; codes are the factory paint codes.

variants.name: Datsun 620 Li'l Hustler (standard bed)

engine.power_note: engine changed by year: L16 1595cc 96 hp (1972-73), L18 1770cc 100 hp (1974), L20B 1952cc 110 hp SAE gross (1975-79; net rating ~97 hp in later years)

variants.transmission: F4W63 4-speed manual (1972-73), F4W71 4-speed manual (1974-79); 3N71 3-speed automatic optional from mid-1972; FS5W71B 5-speed manual optional from 1977
variants.notes: Torsion-bar independent front suspension, leaf-sprung solid rear axle. Length conflict resolved: 4297.7 mm is the short bed; ~4610 mm is the long bed. Prior-recorded wheelbase 2567.9 mm was a conversion artifact; correct short-bed wheelbase is 2545 mm (100.2 in).

citations.note: engine progression L16 96hp/L18 100hp/L20B 110hp by year; wheelbases 2545mm and 2785mm; transmissions incl. FS5W71B 5-speed from 1977; Li'l Hustler name

variants.notes: Long bed introduced in the US for 1975 on the 2785 mm (109.6 in) wheelbase with extra rear overhang, giving a 7-foot bed vs the standard 6-foot.

markets.name: United Kingdom
markets.notes: The 620 was officially imported in small numbers by Datsun UK (Octav Botnar's Worthing-based operation, importing Datsuns since 1968), sold as a commercial 'one-ton pick-up' badged Datsun 1500 rather than under the 620 name. Period UK evidence (Commercial Motor road test of 1 July 1977, show reports of 1978-1979) consistently describes a single spartan variant: long-wheelbase single-cab pickup, 1,483 cc J15 petrol engine, 77 bhp, 1,000 kg payload, four-speed manual, RHD, offered with no option packs. It was always a marginal seller next to Datsun's UK car range; DVLA-derived survivor data (howmanyleft.co.uk lists it as 'DATSUN G620 PICK-UP') shows only a handful remain, and many UK 620s seen today are later personal imports from South Africa, Australia or the US.

variants.name: Datsun 1500 one-ton pick-up (620, long wheelbase G620)
variants.body: 2-door single-cab pickup, long wheelbase, standard drop-side bed, RHD

engine.power_note: Period UK source (Commercial Motor, 14 Sep 1979) quotes 57 kW / 77 bhp from the 1,483 cc engine; Wikipedia gives the J15 as 77 PS (76 hp), so the figure is likely PS/DIN. Confirms UK trucks did NOT get the L16/L18/L20B fitted in North America.

variants.transmission: 4-speed manual (only transmission offered in UK per ex-Datsun UK staff account; no automatic or 5-speed documented)
variants.notes: Payload 1,000 kg ('one-tonner'), hence the commercial positioning. Ex-Datsun UK employee (joined 1976) recalls UK 620s came in three colours only (blue, red, white) with no option packs. Commercial Motor road-tested the Datsun one-ton pick-up on 1 July 1977 and still listed the 1,483 cc one-tonner in its September 1979 lightweights survey; King Cab and 4WD were never UK-market 620 offerings (the 'UK only received the long wheelbase 1-Tonner with L18 or 4WD 2.2 King Cab' line in Wikipedia's Datsun Truck article describes the later 720). Prior collected data (wheelbase 2567.9 mm, J15 77 hp from en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datsun_Truck) mixed the correct UK engine with SHORT-wheelbase (2,545 mm/100.2 in) dimensions; the UK one-tonner was the long-wheelbase chassis, 2,785 mm (109.6 in). UK survivors: a 1978 T-registration UK truck documented on Retro Rides; a 1974 example auctioned by Hampson was a South African museum import, not UK-supplied. Colours per the ex-Datsun UK staff account: sold in three colours only (blue, red, white) with no options; formal paint names, codes and shades unknown, hence placeholder swatches.

citations.note: Period UK trade press: Datsun one-tonne pick-up, 1,000 kg payload, 57 kW (77 bhp) 1,483 cc engine; references CM road test of 1.7.77
1978 UK show report: Datsun exhibited a one-ton pick-up with 1,483 cc petrol engine
Ex-Datsun UK (1976) staff account: UK 620s in three colours (blue/red/white), no options, 4-speed manual, 1500 cc petrol; article notes Datsun was the largest Japanese brand in the UK at the time

620 built Feb 1972-1979; outside North America most markets received the J15 1.5 (57 kW/77 hp); two wheelbases, long = G620; the L18 1-Tonner / 4WD 2.2 King Cab UK sentence belongs to the 720 successor section
DVLA registration data lists the model as 'DATSUN G620 PICK-UP', confirming long-wheelbase G620 chassis code on UK registrations and present-day scarcity
620 wheelbase figures: 2,545 mm (100.2 in) standard, 2,785 mm (109.6 in) long-wheelbase G/GN620
Surviving UK-registered 1978 (T-plate) Datsun 620 pickup, Sussex — evidence of period UK sales
UK-auctioned 1974 620 explicitly documented as a recent South African import — illustrates that many UK survivors are not UK-supplied trucks

unresolved: Exact UK on-sale start year: 620 production began Feb 1972 and Datsun UK was active from 1968, but earliest UK-specific 620 evidence found is the 1976 staff account and the 1 July 1977 Commercial Motor road test; UK sales may have started 1972-1975 (years.from=1976 is 'earliest confirmed', not launch)
Exact UK sales end: CM still listed the 1,483 cc one-tonner in Sep 1979; whether stock sold into 1980 before the 720 replaced it is unverified

Official UK model name on price lists: badged 'Datsun 1500' / sold as 'one-ton pick-up'; whether literature said 'Datsun 1500 Pick-up' vs 'Datsun 1 Ton Pick-Up' unverified (the '1 Ton Pick-Up' name is confirmed in CM only from 1981, i.e. 720 era)

Overall length/width/height and kerb weight for the UK long-wheelbase spec: prior data (width 1587.5, height 1544.3, wheelbase 2567.9) appears to be generic short-wheelbase US-conversion figures and was not confirmed for the UK truck; only the 2,785 mm LWB wheelbase is supported
Bed length: US long-wheelbase trucks had a ~7 ft bed, but UK one-tonner bed dimensions unverified
Whether any UK 620 was sold with the L18 engine: no period evidence found; all period UK sources say 1,483 cc (the L18 '1-Tonner' reference relates to the 720)
Full text/figures of the Commercial Motor 1 July 1977 road test (kerb weight, GVW, dimensions) could not be retrieved via search excerpts

Factory colour names/codes: the 1971/1973/1974 Datsun UK colour charts (jdmjunkies scans, archived in data/research/) cover the passenger-car range only; the commercial pickup is not listed. Best evidence remains the staff recollection of blue/red/white.

markets.name: Australia
markets.notes: In Australia the 620 was sold from 1972 to 1979 badged simply as the 'Datsun 1500' ute (numeric model-variant badging was not used locally), powered by the 1483cc J15 engine rather than the L-series units fitted in North America. It was offered as a single-cab ute, with wellside and flat/tray-back bodies known, and a small number of long-wheelbase examples exist locally. Passenger Datsuns were assembled at Clayton (Motor Producers) in this era, but no source confirms local assembly of the 620, which appears to have been fully imported from Japan.

variants.name: Datsun 1500 ute (620) single cab, wellside

engine.power_note: 57 kW (77 PS) at 5200 rpm, 118 Nm at 3200 rpm; the OHV J15 (also used in forklifts) was fitted in most non-US markets including Australia, badged 'Datsun 1500'

variants.transmission: 4-speed manual (CarsGuide lists Single Cab 1.5L 4 SP Manual; bench seat with floor-shift cut-out documented)
variants.notes: Called 'Datsun 1500' in Australia because numeric variant badging was not used; the 'Hustler' name used elsewhere was avoided due to the Hillman Hustler already on the Australian market. New price range AUD $2,640-$4,070 (CarsGuide). Sold Jan 1972 to Jan 1979 per Australian windscreen fitment data. Dimensions/weight are the 1978 CarsGuide single-cab figures.

citations.note: 1978 AU dimensions: length 4609, width 1590, height 1545, wheelbase 2785, kerb 1065 kg

variants.name: Datsun 1500 ute (620) long-wheelbase
variants.body: 2-door single-cab utility on the longer of the two 620 wheelbases (100.2 in / 109.6 in globally), long bed

engine.power_note: Assumed same J15 as standard AU ute; no AU source shows a different engine

variants.notes: Australian owner-forum (OZDAT) threads document long-wheelbase 620 utes in Australia (e.g. a 1975 620 LWB), consistent with the two global wheelbase lengths, but official AU-market specifications and sales volumes were not found. Treated as a rare/possible AU variant.

variants.name: Datsun 1500 (620) flat/tray-back
variants.body: Single-cab with flat tray body ('flat body' in period brochures) instead of wellside bed

engine.power_note: Assumed same J15 as standard AU ute

variants.notes: A tray-top/'flat body' variant was brochured for the 620 and flat-back Datsun 1500 utes exist in Australia (classifieds and owner accounts), but no AU source explicitly documents a factory cab-chassis-with-tray model line, price, or specs. Could equally be a factory flat body or locally fitted tray on a cab-chassis.

citations.note: Notes a tray top was available and brochured as the 'flat body' for the 620

unresolved: Whether Nissan officially sold a 620 cab-chassis/tray model new in Australia vs trays being aftermarket/coachbuilt
Tray dimensions and tare weight

Local assembly: Motor Producers Ltd (Clayton, VIC) assembled Datsun passenger cars from 1968 and Nissan took it over in 1972 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nissan_Motor_Australia), but no source found stating the 620 ute was assembled in Australia; it was most likely fully imported - unverified either way

1600/1800 badging: no evidence of L16/L18 or 'Datsun 1600/1800' 620 utes sold new in Australia; all AU sources show J15/1500 only (L18 documented for Middle East, L16/L18/L20B for North America per https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datsun_truck)

Diesel: Wikipedia mentions a 2200cc (SD22) diesel in connection with the Australian entry, but other excerpts state the diesel was not imported to Australia; competing claims, treated as not offered in AU

Factory colours: uniquecarsandparts.com.au hosts 1969-1980 Datsun charts but serves a fingerprint bot-wall even to GitHub runners (verified 2026-07-16); needs a human browser session or another source.

markets.name: Japan (JDM)
markets.notes: The 7th-generation Datsun Truck (620) was sold in Japan from February 1972 to 1979 as a 1-ton-class small pickup. Unlike North American models (OHC L16/L18/L20B, optional automatic), home-market trucks carried over the Austin-derived OHV J-series engines (J13 1.3L and J15 1.5L) from the 520/521 and were manual-transmission only. The line-up comprised short- and long-wheelbase single-cab trucks and a 6-seat double cab (Wピック); an extended cab sold domestically as 'Custom' (export name: King Cab) was added in 1976, and a short-lived SD22 diesel appeared in 1978.

variants.name: Datsun Truck 1500 Standard Body (標準ボデー)
variants.body: 2-door single cab, short wheelbase, styleside bed, 2-seater

engine.power_note: 77 PS/5200 rpm, 12.0 kg-m/3200 rpm (JIS gross); base 1300 grade with J13 1.3L 62 PS also offered; SD22 2.2L 65 PS diesel added 1978 (JDM only, short-lived)

variants.transmission: 4-speed manual (column or floor shift); no automatic offered in Japan
variants.notes: 1972 catalog specs for 1500 Standard Deluxe floor-shift (N620T): payload 1000 kg, curb weight 1000 kg, top speed 135 km/h. Sept 1977 minor change added front disc brakes (NA models had them from launch).

citations.note: 1972 JDM catalog: J15 1483cc 77ps/5200rpm, 12.0kg-m/3200rpm, 4MT, length 4300, width 1590, height 1545, WB 2545, bed 1860, payload/curb 1000kg; J-type 1.3L 62ps option
JDM 620 used carried-over J13/J15 OHV engines, 1-ton payload, MT only; Sept 1977 front disc brakes; 1978 SD22 diesel added
1972 standard model: payload 1000kg, bed length 1860mm, 2 seats, top speed 135 km/h

variants.name: Datsun Truck W-Pick / Double Cab (Wピック, ダブルピックアップ)

engine.power_note: 77 PS/5200 rpm JIS gross; double cab was offered only with the 1.5L J15 (not the 1.3L J13)

variants.notes: 6-seat double-cab 'W-Pick' offered from launch (Feb 1972); the light van body of the previous 520 generation was dropped, leaving the W-Pick as the passenger-oriented body. 1972 catalog notes the pickup was available only with the 1500 engine.

variants.name: Datsun Truck Custom (カスタム) - extended cab, export name King Cab
variants.body: 2-door extended cab (cab lengthened 150 mm with small rear side windows), styleside bed

engine.power_note: 77 PS/5200 rpm JIS gross (JDM extended cab kept the J15; the L20B King Cab was a North American model)

variants.notes: A JDM extended-cab 620 did exist, but it was sold domestically as 'Custom' rather than 'King Cab' (King Cab was the export/US name). Added in 1976 (the year Kyushu plant 620 production started, Dec 23 1976), with a 150 mm longer cab, reclining seats, car-like equipment and side moldings. Widely photographed JDM 'King Cab' survivors are typically re-imported US-spec L20B trucks.

citations.note: 1976: extended-cab model with reclining seats added, domestic name Custom, export name King Cab; cab extended 150 mm with small side windows (per 1977 catalog reference); Kyushu plant production from Dec 23 1976
King Cab name/history context; US-market 620 King Cab (1977+) with L20B, AT and disc brakes was a North American product
Aug 1977 JDM catalog (No.7081K): Custom cab extended by 150 mm with small side windows

unresolved: No JDM catalog dimensions, curb weight or payload found for the Custom extended cab

Exact launch month in 1976 vs. early 1977 not pinned down
Factory colours: no market-specific colour documentation surfaced (2026-07-16 research round); needs period brochures

markets.name: South Africa
markets.notes: In South Africa the 620 was sold without a model number, simply as the 'Datsun One-Ton Pick-Up', and was renamed 'Datsun 680' with the 1978 facelift. Datsun-Nissan assembled CKD Datsuns at the Rosslyn plant near Pretoria (full-scale manufacturing from 1973) and was the country's top-selling marque in 1976-1978, but per-model assembly confirmation for the 620 is scarce in online sources. Unlike the half-ton Datsun 1200/1400 (B140), which is a separate vehicle built locally until 2008, the 620/680 did NOT have an extended local run: SA sources describe the 680 as 'not so successful' and it was replaced by the Datsun 720 in 1980.

variants.name: Datsun One-Ton Pick-Up (620)

engine.power_note: Not verified for SA. Wikipedia's generic statement 'in most of the world the 620 was equipped with the J15 (57 kW / 77 hp)' is the likely origin of the prior J15/77hp record, but no SA-specific source confirms which engine(s) were fitted locally; J-series (J15/J16) and L-series (L16/L18) were all offered in various export markets.

variants.notes: Marketed on its nominal one-ton (approx. 1000 kg) payload class, hence the market name; a rating the prior recorded payload of 653.6 kg contradicts (that figure matches a US half-ton spec converted from pounds). The prior width/height/wheelbase values (1587.5 / 1544.3 / 2567.9 mm) are inch-to-mm conversion artifacts from US-market data, not SA-verified.

citations.note: 620 sold in South Africa as the 'Datsun One-Ton Pick-Up'; global 620 production Feb 1972-1979; J15 77 hp described only as the 'most of the world' engine, not SA-specific
Rosslyn plant opened 1966 assembling CKD Datsun models; company renamed Datsun Nissan South Africa Ltd in 1973; Datsun was SA's leading brand 1976-1978
Confirms the long-running (1971-2008) locally built Datsun 1200/1400 half-ton bakkie is a separate model, not the 620

unresolved: SA engine fitment: J15 (77 hp) vs J16 vs L16/L18 — no SA-specific source found; prior J15 claim traces to Wikipedia's generic non-SA sentence
Whether the 620 was assembled CKD at Rosslyn: Rosslyn assembled 'mainly Datsun models' from 1966 and the 1200 bakkie line is confirmed, but no source explicitly names the 620/One-Ton as Rosslyn-built
Exact SA introduction year (global launch Feb 1972; SA on-sale date unverified)
All dimensions, weight, transmission, and exact payload rating (nominal 'one ton' per market name, but no rated figure found)

variants.name: Datsun 680 (facelifted 620)

engine.power_note: Unverified. Its successor, the SA-market Datsun 720 of 1980, used the 1.8-litre OHC L18, suggesting L-series availability locally by then, but no source states the 680's own engine.

variants.notes: The 1978 facelift (new grille, front bumper, tracks widened 30 mm) coincided with the SA rename from 'One-Ton Pick-Up' to 'Datsun 680'. SA retrospective coverage calls the 680 'not so successful' against the Toyota Hilux; it was replaced by the Datsun 720 in 1980, so the SA 620/680 had no extended local production run after the global model ended in 1979.

markets.name: Europe (continental)
markets.notes: The Datsun 620 (1972-1979) was sold in continental Europe primarily as a light 1-tonne-class commercial pickup, with documented period presence in Germany, the Netherlands and France (German Wikipedia entry, Dutch parts specialists covering 'Datsun Pickup 620 1972-1979', and French Datsun club/technical archives). Unlike North America, which got L16/L18/L20B engines, European (and most rest-of-world) trucks used the 1.5-litre J15 OHV petrol engine with 77 PS, in LHD single-cab form with 4-speed manual gearbox. Scandinavian and Swiss sales, exact local badging ('1500'/'1600'), and any official European King Cab offering could not be verified from available sources.

variants.name: Datsun 620 Pick-up (J15, single cab)
variants.body: 2-door single cab pickup, LHD; short-bed standard (long-bed/second wheelbase existed in the model range but Europe-specific availability unverified)

engine.power_note: 77 PS / 57 kW, OHV carbureted inline-4; top speed approx. 135 km/h. German sources quote 1471 cm3 but Nissan J15 references give 1483 cc (78 x 77.6 mm).

variants.transmission: 4-speed manual standard (column shift common in Europe); 3-speed automatic existed in the 620 range but European availability unverified
variants.notes: Sold as a light commercial in the roughly 1-tonne payload class (pick-up-trucks.de cites payload up to about 1,000 kg depending on configuration); widely used as a work vehicle in the Netherlands. Width/height/wheelbase figures are for the standard-wheelbase 620 body shared across J15 markets (CarsGuide lists 1590/1545 mm for the 1975-76 J15-engined 620); they were not confirmed from a continental-European homologation document. Prior recorded values width 1587.5 / height 1544.3 / wheelbase 2567.9 appear to be inch-to-mm conversions (62.5 in / 60.8 in / 101.1 in) and the 2567.9 wheelbase conflicts with the commonly cited 2545 mm short wheelbase.

citations.note: 620 built Feb 1972-1979; outside North America equipped with J15, 77 hp (57 kW), ~135 km/h; two wheelbases; King Cab from 1977; 2.2L diesel later in many markets
German-market Wikipedia entry: 620 produced 1972-1979, four-cylinder 57 kW / 77 PS (listed there as 1471 cm3), 135 km/h; confirms German-language market documentation
German reference site: J15 57 kW/77 PS, payload around 1,000 kg depending on configuration, SD22 diesel 48 kW/65 PS offered in some markets
J15 displacement 1,483 cc, 78 x 77.6 mm bore/stroke, 8.3:1 CR, 77 PS (57 kW)
Dutch Nissan/Datsun parts specialist with dedicated Datsun Pickup 620 1972-1979 catalog section, supporting Netherlands market presence
French Datsun club model page and technical manual archive for the Pickup 620 (1972 and 1977 manuals), supporting French market presence
620 dimensions for J15-engined market version: width 1590 mm, height 1545 mm (length listed 4609 mm for that market's body); used as proxy for shared body dimensions
Period recollection that European-delivered (UK dealer, 1976) 620s came only with 1500 cc petrol engine and 4-speed manual
1976 620 re-imported to Germany from Greece; European survivors are typically re-imports
German owners unable to source official period technical data, confirming EU-market spec scarcity

unresolved: Exact continental-European badging: side badges read 'DATSUN 1600' or plain 'DATSUN' per Wikipedia (US-oriented); whether European J15 trucks were badged 'Datsun 1500' (as in Australia) or simply 'Datsun Pick-up' was not confirmed
Scandinavian (Sweden/Norway/Denmark) and Swiss market sales: no market-specific source surfaced; presence unverified
Overall length and bed length for European-spec trucks: 4609 mm appears on CarsGuide for the Australian J15 version; European short-bed figure unverified, so length left null
Kerb weight: ~1,036 kg cited for US L-series trucks; no European J15 kerb weight found, left null

Wheelbase: 2545 mm (short) / 2785 mm (long) commonly cited for the 620 range vs. prior recorded 2567.9 mm (=101.1 in), which looks like a converted US figure; European trucks assumed short-wheelbase but not proven
Whether the L16/L18 petrol engines were ever officially offered in continental Europe (Middle East got L18; Europe evidence points to J15 only)

King Cab in Europe: introduced 1977 but all sales evidence found is North American; a King Cab pictured at a Dutch classic dealer (ruylclassics.nl, April 1979 truck) is most plausibly a later US import - no evidence of official European King Cab sales
Second research round (2026-07-16, ~25 searches incl. German/Dutch/French/Scandinavian) found no period European price lists, brochures or road tests; length, weight and badging remain open

variants.name: Datsun 620 Pick-up Diesel (SD22)

engine.power_note: Sources give 65 PS (48 kW) at 4,000 rpm; English Wikipedia states 49 kW / 66 hp

variants.notes: English Wikipedia says a 2.2 L diesel was 'later made available in many markets' for the 620, and German-language sources (de.wikipedia, pick-up-trucks.de) describe the SD22 diesel option in the 620 context, suggesting European relevance; however, no source explicitly confirming continental European sales of the 620 diesel or its introduction year was found. The SD22 is better documented in the successor 720.

citations.note: 2.2 L diesel with 49 kW (66 hp) later made available in many markets for the 620
German reference: Nissan SD22 inline-4 diesel, 48 kW / 65 PS at 4,000 rpm, offered for some markets
SD22 is a 2,164 cc naturally aspirated inline-4 diesel, 65 PS (48 kW) at 4,000 rpm

unresolved: Whether the SD22 diesel 620 was actually sold in continental Europe, and from which model year (SD22 saloon use began June 1977; some sources associate SD22 pickups mainly with the 1980-83 Datsun 720)
Power rating discrepancy: 65 PS/48 kW vs 66 hp/49 kW
All dimensions, weight and transmission details for the diesel variant
Factory colours: no market-specific colour documentation surfaced (2026-07-16 research round); needs period brochures
